moveAnnotations.annotation: White opens with the queen's pawn, claiming the center. Against the King's Indian, this move takes on special significance because White is going to try to build a massive pawn center while Black lets them do it, planning to attack it later. The whole KID is a philosophical debate: is a big center a strength or a target?
plans: Build a broad center with c4 and e4
alternatives: e4 -- leads to different openings entirely

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays the Indian move order, developing the knight and controlling e4. Rather than contesting the center with ...d5 immediately, Black says 'go ahead, build your center -- I'll attack it later.' This is the hypermodern approach: let your opponent overextend in the center, then strike at the foundation. Nf6 prevents e4 for now and keeps Black's options completely open.
plans: Play ...g6 and ...Bg7 for the King's Indian setup
Let White build the center, then attack it
alternatives: d5 -- the classical approach, meeting the center head-on

moveAnnotations.annotation: White grabs more central space, now controlling d5. Together with d4, White has pawns on c4 and d4 which is the ideal setup in Queen's Pawn openings. White is daring Black to allow e4, which would create a truly massive center. The c4 pawn also prepares Nc3, which will add more pressure to the center.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White's c4+d4 broad center is the foundation of their strategy
plans: Play Nc3 and e4 for maximum central control

moveAnnotations.annotation: This is the defining move of the King's Indian Defense. Black prepares to fianchetto the bishop on g7, where it becomes a monster aiming at the center and the queenside along the long diagonal. The g6 pawn also creates a solid kingside shelter for castling. Black is deliberately allowing White to build a big center because the Bg7 will put enormous pressure on d4 from the flank.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black's kingside fianchetto with g6 creates a fortress but gives White central space
plans: Fianchetto with Bg7, castle, then strike with ...e5 or ...c5
alternatives: d5 -- the Grunfeld, challenging the center immediately with the bishop already committed to flank development

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the knight to c3, controlling d5 and e4. This knight is a critical piece in the KID -- it supports the e4 advance and helps maintain the pawn center. On c3, the knight is beautifully placed to support White's central strategy. The stage is set for e4 on the next move.
plans: Play e4 to build the full center
The knight supports d5 and e4

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black completes the fianchetto. The bishop on g7 is the pride of Black's position -- it's a sniper aimed along the h8-a1 diagonal, putting indirect pressure on d4. Once Black plays ...e5, this bishop becomes incredibly powerful as it aligns with the center. In many KID lines, this bishop single-handedly holds Black's position together while enabling kingside attacks.
plans: The Bg7 pressures d4 and supports a future ...e5 break
Castle next to complete kingside development
alternatives: Already committed to the KID setup -- this is the only consistent move

moveAnnotations.annotation: White achieves the dream setup: pawns on c4, d4, and e4. This is a massive center that controls almost every important square. Most players would be terrified to allow this, but King's Indian players welcome it. The theory is that this center is overextended and can be attacked with ...e5 or ...c5. White's center is strong but also a potential liability if Black can undermine it.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White's c4-d4-e4 pawn center is the maximum possible central presence
plans: Use the space advantage for piece development and queenside expansion
alternatives: This is the main line -- any other move is less ambitious

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black prepares ...e5, the key central break. The d6 pawn supports e5 and also prevents White from playing e5 themselves. This is a crucial preparatory move -- Black can't play ...e5 without d6 because the pawn would hang. The d6 pawn also keeps the position flexible; Black's setup is incredibly resilient and hard to breach despite giving up so much center.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black's d6 pawn supports the upcoming ...e5 central challenge
plans: Play ...e5 to challenge White's center
Castle and complete the KID setup
alternatives: O-O -- castling first is also possible, but d6 is needed before ...e5

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the last minor piece to its natural square. The knight on f3 supports d4 and e4, preparing to castle. White has a textbook classical setup. The question now is how to continue after Black plays ...e5 -- the answer usually involves d5, closing the center and starting a race where White attacks on the queenside and Black attacks on the kingside.
plans: Castle and maintain the center
After ...e5, decide between dxe5, d5, or maintaining tension
alternatives: Be2 -- developing the bishop first, also standard

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black castles immediately, getting the king safe before the central conflict begins. The king on g8 is well-protected behind the g6-f7 pawns and the Bg7. Crucially, the rook comes to f8, where it will support ...f5, which is one of Black's most important attacking ideas in the KID. Everything in Black's setup is geared toward a kingside attack.
plans: Play ...e5, then ...f5 for a kingside attack
The Rf8 supports the ...f5 break

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the bishop to e2, a solid choice. The bishop doesn't go to the more aggressive d3 because it would block the d-file, and in KID positions the d-file becomes important after d5. On e2, the bishop supports castling, can later swing to f3 or d3 if needed, and keeps the position flexible. It's not flashy, but it's the most reliable square.
plans: Castle next, then decide on a plan based on Black's setup
alternatives: Bd3 -- more active but blocks the d-file in the main line structures

moveAnnotations.annotation: The key moment! Black strikes at the center with ...e5, challenging White's d4 pawn. This is the move the entire King's Indian is built around. After d5, the position locks up and the game becomes a race: White pushes on the queenside with c5, Black attacks on the kingside with ...f5, ...g5, and eventually ...f4. It's like two armies fighting on different flanks.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: After d5, a locked center creates a classic KID pawn structure with opposite-flank attacks
plans: After d5, launch a kingside attack with ...f5, ...Nf6-h5-f4
The Bg7 comes alive pressuring d4 from behind
alternatives: Nc6 -- developing first before ...e5, but the immediate break is the main line

moveAnnotations.annotation: White castles before the center closes. Getting the king safe is critical because the game is about to become incredibly sharp. The rook on f1 will also be useful for supporting f3 or swinging to the queenside. White is now ready for d5, which will lock the center and start the classic KID race.
plans: Play d5 to lock the center, then expand on the queenside with c5, a4, b4
alternatives: d5 -- closing the center immediately is also played

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the knight, adding pressure to d4. This is a key move -- the knight on c6 forces White's hand regarding the center. White almost has to play d5 now, because the d4 pawn is attacked twice and defended only once (after ...exd4, Nxd4 Nxd4 isn't ideal for White). The knight will get kicked to e7 after d5, but from there it reroutes to the kingside for the attack.
plans: Force d5, then reroute the knight via e7 to g6 or f5 for the kingside attack
alternatives: Nbd7 -- keeping the knight out of the way of d5, but Nc6 forces the issue more concretely

moveAnnotations.annotation: White closes the center, and the classic KID structure is established. This is one of the most theoretically important positions in chess. The center is locked, and the game becomes a race: White will attack on the queenside (c5, a4, b4, Nd3-b5) while Black attacks on the kingside (...f5, ...f4, ...g5, ...Rf6-h6). It's a pure battle of who gets there first.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Locked center: White's c4-d5-e4 chain vs Black's d6-e5. Opposite-side attacks begin.
plans: Expand on the queenside with c5, a4, b4
Win the race against Black's kingside attack
alternatives: dxe5 -- opening the center is possible but gives Black excellent piece play with the Bg7

moveAnnotations.annotation: The knight retreats to e7, clearing c6 for other pieces and heading for the kingside. This is the standard KID maneuver -- the knight will go to f5 or g6, or support ...f5 from e7. It looks like a retreat but it's actually redeployment: the knight was doing nothing useful on c6 after d5 blocked the center. On e7, it's a flexible piece ready to jump into the attack.
plans: Reroute to g6, f5, or d7 depending on the position
Support ...f5 from e7
alternatives: Na5 -- attacking c4 immediately, a sideline but less common

moveAnnotations.annotation: White reroutes the knight from f3. This looks weird but it's a very deep idea. The knight is heading to d3, where it supports both c5 (the queenside advance) and f4 (preventing Black's kingside attack). From d3, the knight is perfectly placed for White's strategic needs. It also clears f3 for the f-pawn, which might need to support e4 with f3 if Black's pieces pressure it.
plans: Reroute Nd3, supporting c5 and controlling f4
Clear f3 for the f-pawn if needed
alternatives: Nd2 -- another regrouping option, heading for c5 via b3

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black reroutes the knight from f6. This is another classic KID maneuver -- the knight leaves f6 to make room for ...f5, which is the key attacking break. The knight on d7 can go to f6 later (after ...f5) or to c5 to pressure e4. Every piece in Black's army is reorganizing for the kingside assault. The f6 square needs to be clear so the f-pawn can advance.
plans: Clear f6 for the ...f5 break
Reroute Nd7-f6 or Nd7-c5 depending on the position
alternatives: f5 -- playing the break immediately is possible, but Nd7 first improves the knight placement

moveAnnotations.annotation: White plays f3 to solidify the e4 pawn and create a fortress-like structure. The f3 pawn prevents any ...Ng4 or ...Nf5-e3 ideas and makes e4 rock-solid. This also prepares g4, which would stop Black's ...f5 break entirely. White's plan is clear: lock up the kingside, then steamroll on the queenside. This is the classical approach to fighting the KID.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White's f3 creates an ironclad e4 support but commits to a closed kingside structure
plans: Support e4 permanently, prepare queenside expansion with b4 and c5
Consider g4 to prevent ...f5
alternatives: Nd3 -- continuing the knight rerouting, also standard

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black launches the kingside attack! This is THE move of the King's Indian Defense. The f5 pawn crashes into White's center, threatening ...f4 which would seal the kingside and create a devastating attack along the g-file after ...g5 and ...Rf6-g6. If White takes exf5, Black recaptures with ...gxf5 and gets an open g-file aimed at White's king. The race is officially on -- whoever gets there first wins. This is why the King's Indian is one of the most exciting openings in chess.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: ...f5 creates the classic KID attack structure; after ...f4 Black gets a kingside pawn storm
plans: Play ...f4 to seal the kingside, then ...g5, ...h5, ...Rf6-g6 for a mating attack
If exf5, recapture ...gxf5 and use the open g-file
alternatives: a5 -- preventing White's b4, a slower approach but sometimes necessary
